periods.name: Today
periods.detailed: A chance of showers and thunderstorms between 7am and 11am, then a chance of showers and thunderstorms between 11am and noon, then a slight chance of showers and thunderstorms between noon and 2pm. Partly sunny, with a high near 75. Southwest wind 9 to 14 mph. Chance of precipitation is 30%. New rainfall amounts less than a tenth of an inch possible.

periods.name: Tonight
periods.detailed: Partly cloudy, with a low around 56. West wind 6 to 13 mph.

periods.name: Friday

periods.name: Friday Night

key_messages: Lingering showers are expected today with isolated thunderstorms possible as a cold front moves through, then the weather pattern becomes drier for the latter half of the week.
Quiet weather and increasing temperatures are expected this upcoming weekend before another system bring showers and thunderstorms to the region early next week.
afd.what_changed: As of 527 AM EDT Wednesday... Have sent out an early morning update to adjust probability of precipitation and weather over the next few hours based on high resolution models that are performing well in comparison with the radar imagery and automated reports across the region. Today, we're thinking about 15-25% chance of precip in the St. Lawrence Valley, 50-80% chance of precip on the northern and western slopes of the Adirondacks and northern Greens, 5-30% chance of precip in the southern Champlain and Connecticut River valleys, and 30-50% elsewhere. Will be a mix of showers and embedded thunderstorms, drying out by the end of the day.
afd.raw:
000
FXUS61 KBTV 221752
AFDBTV

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Burlington VT
1253 PM EDT Wed Jul 22 2026

.WHAT HAS CHANGED...
As of 527 AM EDT Wednesday...

Have sent out an early morning update to adjust probability of
precipitation and weather over the next few hours based on high
resolution models that are performing well in comparison with
the radar imagery and automated reports across the region.
Today, we're thinking about 15-25% chance of precip in the St. 
Lawrence Valley, 50-80% chance of precip on the northern and 
western slopes of the Adirondacks and northern Greens, 5-30% 
chance of precip in the southern Champlain and Connecticut River
valleys, and 30-50% elsewhere. Will be a mix of showers and
embedded thunderstorms, drying out by the end of the day.

&&

.KEY MESSAGES...
As of 225 AM EDT Wednesday...

1. Lingering showers are expected today with isolated 
thunderstorms possible as a cold front moves through, then the 
weather pattern becomes drier for the latter half of the week. 

2. Quiet weather and increasing temperatures are expected this 
upcoming weekend before another system bring showers and 
thunderstorms to the region early next week.

&&

.DISCUSSION...
As of 225 AM EDT Wednesday...

KEY MESSAGE 1: A warm front is crossing northern New York and Vermont 
early this morning, bringing a few additional showers to the area. 
So far about 0.50-1.00 inches of rainfall has fallen in a swath from 
the St. Lawrence River to the northern Adirondacks and northern Lake 
Champlain. A cold front is expected to cross the region this 
morning, bringing additional showers and thunderstorms. Probability 
of precipitation in a given location is around 50-80% in the western 
Adirondacks, St. Lawrence Valley, and far northern Vermont. 
Probability of precipitation is lowest in the souther Champlain and 
Connecticut River valleys, around 5-30%. Additional anticipated 
rainfall amounts range around 0.10-0.30 inches through the end of 
today, though higher precip amounts and gusty winds could occur in 
any thunderstorms. Outside of storms, southwesterly winds are also 
anticipated throughout the day today with gusts as high as 20-30 
knots likely. The cold front should keep temperatures from reaching 
too high today, particularly in northern New York, where highs will 
be in the 60s west of the Champlain Valley. In the Champlain Valley 
and remainder of Vermont, temperatures are forecast in the mid and 
upper 70s, warmest in the Connecticut River Valley where the cold 
front will visit last. Depending on the speed of the cold front, 
temperatures could end up being a degree or two warmer or colder.
Wednesday night through Friday night will be dry with high pressure 
in control. Humidity levels will be comfortable with highs in the 
70s to lower 80s Thursday and upper 70s to mid 80s Friday. Lows will 
fall into the 40s and 50s each night, with radiational fog potential 
in the typical valley locations.

KEY MESSAGE 2: High pressure starts to shift to the east by the second 
half of the weekend, leading to increasing S/SW flow. The result 
will be warming temperatures, with highs to be mainly in the 
mid/upper 80s both Saturday and Sunday. Humidity doesn't look too 
bad  as dewpoints are expected to remain the upper 50s to mid 60s. 
Our next chance of precipitation arrives late Sunday into Monday and 
likely persists into mid week as upper troughiness sets up across 
eastern Canada and the northeastern CONUS. Shortwaves rotating 
around the upper trough will bring periods of showers and 
thunderstorms. These sorts of features are typically difficult to 
nail down in timing and strength this far out, and indeed, model 
solutions differ quite a bit from Sunday night onward. Have stayed 
with the NBM/WPC forecast for now given the uncertainty, which 
results in chances of showers/thunderstorms each day. Temperatures 
look to remain seasonable through the period.

&&

.AVIATION /12Z WEDNESDAY THROUGH SUNDAY/...
A few light showers associated with a cold front are continuing to 
pass through the region, but little to no terminal impacts are 
expected. Ceilings will bounce between scattered and broken 3000-
7000ft agl through the rest of the afternoon, before generally 
becoming more scattered into the evening. Winds from the west 
southwest will continue to be breezy between15 and 25 knots, with 
pockets of 25-30 knots at MSS/BTV/PBG from channeled flow and 
briefly for MPV/EFK/RUT potentially as the front moves through. 
Winds will weaken slowly after sunset, but some flow at the surface 
will help scour most of the low level moisture away from most 
terminals. Winds may briefly trend towards variable to calm near 
sunrise at SLK/MPV/EFK which may be enough time for a thin layer of 
fog to develop with clearing skies. While confidence is low, have 
trended this time frame towards potential fog with a SCT008 and 3SM 
BR line around 09Z.

Outlook...

Thursday: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Thursday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Friday: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Friday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Saturday: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Saturday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Sunday: VFR. Slight chance SHRA, Slight chance TSRA.

&&

.MARINE...
A Lake Wind Advisory is in effect for Lake Champlain through the
remainder of this afternoon. Westerly winds of 20-30 kts,
following a cold front, will persist through late afternoon,
with waves on the broad lake building to 2-4 feet. Some gusts to
35 kts are possible, mainly across the broad portion of Lake
Champlain. Should see winds gradually diminish this evening and
into the overnight hours. 

&&

.BTV WATCHES/WARNINGS/ADVISORIES...
VT...None.
NY...None.

&&

$$

WHAT HAS CHANGED...Storm
DISCUSSION...Storm/Hastings
AVIATION...Danzig
MARINE...Banacos/NWS BTV

air.discussion: On Wednesday, July 22nd, expect a chance for some low-moderate fine particulate matter pollution in Vermont as residual wildfire smoke continues to circulate in the northeast US and Canada.
